title_en: Chapter on the Explanation of Tawhid and the Testimony of Faith
title_ar: باب تفسير التوحيد وشهادة أن لا إله إلا الله
content_en:
This chapter provides a sustained theological explanation of what the declaration 'La ilaha illallah' actually means and what it demands of the one who utters it. It is perhaps the most doctrinally dense chapter in the early portion of Kitab al-Tawhid, and it addresses a widespread misconception that persisted in the author's time — and persists in ours — namely, the belief that mere utterance of the Shahada without understanding its meaning constitutes Tawhid.

Ibn Abd al-Wahhab draws upon the verse: 'Those whom they call upon do not possess the power of intercession — only those who bear witness to the truth (i.e., to Tawhid) and who know it' (Quran 43:86). The phrase 'and who know it' is decisive: the Shahada must be accompanied by knowledge (ilm) of what it affirms and what it negates. The scholars of Ahl al-Sunnah have identified seven conditions for the Shahada to be valid and efficacious: knowledge (ilm), certainty (yaqin), acceptance (qabul), submission (inqiyad), truthfulness (sidq), sincerity (ikhlas), and love (mahabbah) — some scholars add an eighth: rejection of all that is worshipped besides Allah (kufr bil-taghut).

The theological core of this chapter is the proper understanding of the word 'ilah'. In Arabic, 'ilah' does not simply mean 'creator' or 'provider' — it means 'one who is worshipped'. Thus, the declaration 'La ilaha illallah' means: 'There is no one worthy of worship except Allah.' The early Arab polytheists of Mecca did not deny that Allah was the creator — the Quran itself records their acknowledgment of this: 'If you ask them who created them, they will surely say: Allah' (Quran 43:87). Their error was in the practical dimension: they worshipped other deities alongside Allah, believing these intermediaries would bring them closer to Allah. Islam came to demolish precisely this logic, establishing that worship — in all its forms — belongs exclusively to Allah.

This chapter also engages with the famous story of the death of Abu Talib, the Prophet's uncle, who protected Muhammad ﷺ throughout his years in Mecca. Despite the Prophet's ﷺ earnest desire for his uncle to accept Islam, Abu Talib died refusing to say 'La ilaha illallah', out of fear of social criticism from his peers. This narrative illustrates that the barrier to Tawhid is often not intellectual — Abu Talib was well acquainted with the truth of the Prophet's message — but one of pride, social allegiance, and the love of worldly regard over divine acceptance.

Ibn Abd al-Wahhab places great emphasis on the 'nafi wa ithbat' (negation and affirmation) structure of the Shahada. The 'La ilaha' (there is no deity) negates all false objects of worship — idols, saints, rulers, desires, or anything else that the human heart might elevate to the level of ultimate sovereignty. The 'illallah' (except Allah) then affirms that this absolute status of worthiness of worship belongs to Allah alone. Without both elements — the negation and the affirmation — the Shahada is incomplete. A person who affirms Allah without rejecting the taghut has not fulfilled Tawhid; and conceptually, one cannot truly affirm Allah without simultaneously rejecting everything that falsely claims His station.
